sources.issuing_organisation: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery Foundation
sources.exact_document_title: Updated Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Otitis Externa — Official Fact Sheet and Key Action Statements
sources.version: 2014 update; current AAO-HNSF acute otitis externa guideline
sources.population: People aged 2 years or older with diffuse acute otitis externa.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, emergency, paediatric, internal-medicine, and otolaryngology settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for diffuse acute otitis externa symptoms, modifiers, and key actions; partial for broad ear pain and not validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Fact sheet — AOE symptoms and modifying factors
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports swimmer’s-ear terminology, water exposure, ear-canal inflammation, itching, fullness, hearing change, external-ear pain or tenderness, diabetes, immunocompromise, and tympanic-membrane context.

exact_sections.heading: Fact sheet — significant guideline action statements
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports distinguishing AOE from other otalgia or otorrhoea, pain assessment, clinician-selected topical management, administration discussion, and reassessment after non-response.

sources.issuing_organisation: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery Foundation
sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Practice Guideline: Hoarseness (Dysphonia) (Update) – official fact sheet and key action statements
sources.population: Patients of all ages with dysphonia or hoarseness.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care and otolaryngology assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for dysphonia assessment and escalation; referral pathways require UAE clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Fact sheet questions 2–7
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines dysphonia and hoarseness, describes voice-quality changes, common causes, vocal demand, tobacco exposure, reflux context, and prevention topics.

exact_sections.heading: Key action statements: assessment, escalation, and laryngoscopy
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires history and examination for modifying factors and expedited laryngeal evaluation for neck mass, respiratory distress or stridor, tobacco history, recent procedures, and persistent symptoms.

exact_sections.heading: Key action statements: education and outcomes
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented preventive discussion, questions, follow-up, and change in voice or quality of life.

sources.issuing_organisation: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery Foundation
sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Practice Guideline (Update): Earwax (Cerumen Impaction)
sources.population: People older than 6 months with cerumen impaction, excluding specified ear-canal, radiation, and prior ear-surgery contexts.
sources.clinical_setting: Any setting in which cerumen impaction is identified, monitored, or managed.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected cerumen symptoms, otoscopy, modifying factors, post-treatment assessment, and qualified referral documentation. Removal procedures, drops, irrigation, treatment, hearing-aid selection, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Scope, definition, and symptoms of cerumen impaction
exact_sections.locator: Full guideline supporting text and Key Action Statement 2A; official full-text lines 183–217 and 417–475
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports cerumen-impaction identity, hearing loss, tinnitus, fullness, pain, discharge, otoscopy, ear-canal or tympanic-membrane visibility, and audiologic-test obstruction context.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnosis and modifying factors
exact_sections.locator: Key Action Statements 2A–2B; official full-text lines 302–305 and 417–528
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports diagnosis only when clinician-observed cerumen is symptomatic or prevents assessment, plus history or examination of management-modifying factors.

exact_sections.heading: Outcomes assessment and referral
exact_sections.locator: Key Action Statements 9–10; official full-text lines 315–316 and 1137 onward
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting resolution after clinician treatment, considering alternate causes when symptoms persist after resolution, and clinician referral after unsuccessful initial management without mapping a procedure or treatment instruction.

sources.exact_document_title: Management of Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline
sources.version: AAOS CPG approved 2024; replaces the 2016 edition
sources.population: Adults aged over 18 with complaints attributable to carpal tunnel syndrome.
sources.clinical_setting: Orthopaedic, hand, primary, and related adult clinical services.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult carpal-tunnel diagnostic terminology and CTS-6 context; partial for a broad wrist and hand pain workflow and not validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Guideline scope and current-edition statement
exact_sections.locator: official CPG page and PDF pages 4–5 and 19–21
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines the current adult carpal-tunnel diagnostic and management population and confirms the 2024 guideline scope.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnosis: CTS-6, ultrasonography, and NCV/EMG
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports carpal-tunnel diagnostic terminology and clinician use of symptom-and-sign history within CTS-6, with selected ultrasound or electrodiagnostic testing when needed.

sources.issuing_organisation: American College of Cardiology, American Heart Association, and Heart Rhythm Society
sources.exact_document_title: 2018 Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay: Guidelines Made Simple
sources.version: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guidelines Made Simple selection of tables and figures
sources.population: Patients with symptoms suggestive of or consistent with bradycardia or cardiac conduction disease.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial, outpatient, acute, and specialist evaluation of suspected or documented bradycardia and conduction disorders.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for clinician-led history, physical examination, ECG and selected test review, symptom-rhythm correlation, reversible-cause context, and monitor selection. It is old, is a condensed official summary, and does not authorize automatic rhythm diagnosis, pacing, treatment, or referral.

exact_sections.heading: Evaluation of Bradycardia and Conduction Disease Algorithm
exact_sections.locator: Guidelines Made Simple PDF page 9, Figure 1, plus page 10 reversible-cause table
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports comprehensive clinician history and physical examination, ECG, directed blood testing, symptom frequency, selected ambulatory monitoring, and reversible or treatable cause context without generating a diagnosis or test order.

exact_sections.heading: Chronic bradycardia and conduction-disorder algorithms
exact_sections.locator: Guidelines Made Simple PDF pages 11-13 and 23-24
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting symptom-rhythm correlation, symptom frequency, structural-disease context, reviewed ECG or ambulatory monitoring, and observed follow-up when appropriate; pacing and treatment branches are not mapped.

sources.exact_document_title: 2024 AHA/ACC Perioperative CV Management Guideline: Key Points
sources.version: Official ACC key-points summary of the 2024 multisociety guideline
sources.population: Adults undergoing noncardiac surgery with cardiovascular disease, cardiovascular risk factors, or symptoms concerning for cardiovascular disease.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative cardiovascular assessment and perioperative planning for noncardiac surgery.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for a stepwise clinician-led cardiovascular risk assessment, selective testing, team-based review, and anticoagulation risk context. It does not support automatic risk scores, tests, medicine changes, bridging, or doses, and UAE pathways remain local.

exact_sections.heading: Key points 1-4 - stepwise cardiovascular assessment, selective testing, and team care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting clinician-led cardiovascular disease and risk-factor review, use of an already completed validated risk assessment, selective testing decisions, and team-based discussion.

exact_sections.heading: Key point 6 - therapeutic anticoagulation and individualized thrombotic or bleeding risk
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting anticoagulant use and clinician-assessed thrombotic and bleeding risk context without mapping hold, continue, bridge, medicine, or dose instructions.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Advisory for Intraoperative Awareness and Brain Function Monitoring
sources.population: Patients receiving general anesthesia, with risk-factor and reported-awareness qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative risk assessment and post-event interview, documentation, and support after a report of intraoperative awareness.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting previous awareness, focused record and interview review, selected risk discussion, post-event structured interview, occurrence reporting, and offered counseling or support. It is old, is not a guideline or standard, and does not establish that awareness occurred.

exact_sections.heading: Preoperative evaluation and Appendix 1 summary
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports review of records, interview about previous anesthesia experiences including prior awareness, focused risk assessment, and informing selected higher-risk patients without predicting awareness.

exact_sections.heading: Postoperative interventions and Appendix 1 summary
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports a structured interview after a reported event, documenting the account, offering counseling or psychological support, and completing an occurrence report without confirming causation.

sources.exact_document_title: Standards for Basic Anesthetic Monitoring
sources.population: Patients receiving general anesthesia, regional anesthesia, or monitored anesthesia care, subject to stated emergency exceptions.
sources.clinical_setting: Operating room and procedural anesthesia monitoring.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting qualified anesthesia personnel and monitored oxygenation, ventilation, circulation, and temperature. It does not generate thresholds, treatment, or emergency conclusions.

exact_sections.heading: Standard I — qualified anesthesia personnel
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting continuous presence of qualified anesthesia personnel during anesthesia care.

exact_sections.heading: Standard II — oxygenation and ventilation
exact_sections.locator: official standard, Standard II oxygenation and ventilation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented oxygenation, inspired gas, pulse oximetry, ventilation, airway-device placement, carbon dioxide, and respiratory monitoring.

exact_sections.heading: Standard II — circulation and body temperature
exact_sections.locator: official standard, Standard II circulation and temperature
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented ECG, blood pressure, heart rate, circulation, and temperature monitoring when clinically significant.

sources.exact_document_title: Basic Standards for Preanesthesia Care
sources.population: Patients receiving anesthesia care, with emergency modification permitted.
sources.clinical_setting: Preanesthesia assessment before anesthesia care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for record review, patient interview, focused examination, previous anesthesia and medical therapy, risk-relevant physical condition, tests, consultations, consent, and documentation. UAE policy and emergency modifications remain local responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Review of the available medical record
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports review of medical, anesthesia, medicine, investigation, and consultation records relevant to anesthesia.

exact_sections.heading: Patient interview and focused examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports previous-anesthesia history, medical therapy, focused examination, airway and physical-condition assessment, and factors affecting perioperative risk.

exact_sections.heading: Tests, consultations, consent, and documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected tests or consultations, consent for anesthesia care, and recording the assessment in the medical record.

sources.exact_document_title: Statement on Documentation of Anesthesia Care
sources.population: Patients receiving anesthesia services across preanesthesia, intraoperative, and postanesthesia phases.
sources.clinical_setting: Anesthesia care continuum and anesthesia medical records.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documentation fields and continuity of anesthesia records. It supports recording clinician-assessed facts and completed plans, not generating management, medicines, doses, procedures, referrals, discharge, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Documentation across the anesthesia care continuum
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports accurate, complete, and legible anesthesia documentation across preanesthesia, intraoperative, and postanesthesia phases.

exact_sections.heading: Preanesthesia documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports identity, disposition, medical, surgical and anesthetic history, medicines, allergies and reactions, fasting, advance directives, airway and cardiopulmonary examination, tests, consultations, ASA status, plan including postanesthesia and pain care, and consent.

exact_sections.heading: Intraoperative or procedural anesthesia documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports re-evaluation, equipment, medicines, staff, monitoring, techniques, positioning, lines and procedures, unusual events, fluids or blood products, output, and transfer status as recorded facts.

exact_sections.heading: Postanesthesia documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports PACU admission and discharge records, discharge criteria, complications, postanesthesia evaluation, and documented recovery status.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Guidelines for the Prevention, Detection, and Management of Respiratory Depression Associated with Neuraxial Opioid Administration
sources.population: Patients receiving neuraxial opioids, with patient-specific respiratory-risk qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Periprocedural and postprocedural neuraxial opioid care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for focused risk history and documented ventilation, oxygenation, and consciousness monitoring. Opioid selection, dose, monitoring frequency, and treatment are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Focused history and physical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting sleep apnea, comorbidity, medicines, previous response, and other respiratory-depression risk factors.

exact_sections.heading: Monitoring for respiratory depression
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented ventilation, oxygenation, and level-of-consciousness assessment without specifying dose or generating treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Guidelines for the Perioperative Management of Patients with Obstructive Sleep Apnea
sources.population: Adult and pediatric patients older than 1 year with suspected or established obstructive sleep apnea in perioperative settings.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative anesthesia care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for record review, patient or family interview, airway examination, prior airway difficulty, OSA symptoms and studies, severity, procedure, and opioid-related risk context. Treatment and disposition recommendations are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Preoperative evaluation for suspected or established OSA
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports record review, patient or family interview, airway examination, prior airway difficulty, OSA symptoms, sleep-study results, and severity documentation.

exact_sections.heading: Determining perioperative risk from OSA
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting OSA severity, procedure invasiveness, postoperative analgesic context, and clinician-assessed perioperative risk without an automatic disposition or treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: Statement on Post-Dural Puncture Headache Management
sources.version: Approved by the ASA House of Delegates on 2021-10-13
sources.population: Predominantly obstetric patients with recognised or suspected unintentional dural puncture or post-dural puncture headache.
sources.clinical_setting: Neuraxial anesthesia follow-up, postpartum assessment, discharge, and post-discharge follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documented headache history, focused examination, atypical or severe features, and follow-up after suspected or confirmed dural puncture. It is only partial for a broad non-obstetric post-spinal-headache workflow, and treatment recommendations are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: PDPH Definition and PDPH Diagnosis
exact_sections.locator: official statement, PDPH Definition and PDPH Diagnosis paragraphs
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the dural-puncture context, headache timing and features, associated symptoms, focused history and examination, and atypical or severe neurological features without automatically diagnosing PDPH.

exact_sections.heading: Follow-up and Summary Recommendations
exact_sections.locator: official statement, Follow-up paragraphs and Summary Recommendations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented follow-up until symptom resolution, brief neurological assessment, and verbal or written contact advice as clinician-completed actions without generating treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Guidelines for Perioperative Blood Management
sources.population: Patients undergoing surgical and other invasive procedures where blood loss, anemia, coagulopathy, or transfusion may be relevant.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative blood-management assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for record review, prior transfusion and reactions, bleeding or thrombotic history, laboratory review, patient preferences, and clinician discussion of blood-product risks and benefits. Transfusion thresholds and management are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Preoperative evaluation for blood management
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports medical-record review, transfusion history, coagulopathy, thrombosis, medicine and comorbidity context, and relevant laboratory results.

exact_sections.heading: Patient preferences and blood-product discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented discussion of blood products, risks, benefits, alternatives, and patient preferences without recommending transfusion.

sources.exact_document_title: Standards for Postanesthesia Care
sources.population: Patients who receive general anesthesia, regional anesthesia, or monitored anesthesia care.
sources.clinical_setting: PACU or equivalent postanesthesia recovery location.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for transfer, arrival evaluation, handover, recovery monitoring and records, discharge criteria, and qualified discharge responsibility. It does not generate discharge or treatment decisions.

exact_sections.heading: Standards I–II — PACU transfer, arrival evaluation, and handover
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports transport with appropriate monitoring and support, arrival re-evaluation, verbal report, and documented transfer status.

exact_sections.heading: Standard III — continual evaluation and recovery record
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documented oxygenation, ventilation, circulation, consciousness, temperature, pain or other recovery observations, and use of a recovery score where appropriate.

exact_sections.heading: Standards IV–V — discharge responsibility and criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports qualified discharge responsibility and institution-approved discharge criteria as recorded decisions, not automatic clearance.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Guidelines for Postanesthetic Care
sources.population: Patients recovering from anesthesia or sedation, with clinical and procedural qualifications.
sources.clinical_setting: Postanesthesia recovery and discharge assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for periodic recovery assessment domains including respiratory and cardiovascular status, mental status, temperature, pain, nausea or vomiting, hydration, urine output, drainage, and bleeding. Treatment recommendations are not mapped; recency is a blocker.

exact_sections.heading: Patient assessment and monitoring during recovery
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports periodic documented respiratory, cardiovascular, neurologic or mental, temperature, pain, nausea or vomiting, hydration, urine-output, drainage, and bleeding observations.

exact_sections.heading: Discharge assessment and documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment and documentation of discharge readiness and responsible discharge processes without automatic discharge.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Advisory for Preanesthesia Evaluation: An Updated Report by the American Society of Anesthesiologists Task Force on Preanesthesia Evaluation
sources.population: Patients of all ages scheduled for elective general anesthesia, regional anesthesia, or moderate or deep sedation.
sources.clinical_setting: Preanesthesia history, physical examination, selective testing, and consultation for elective procedures.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for medical-record review, patient interview, directed airway, lung and heart examination, and selective testing including pregnancy testing when results would alter management. It is an older advisory, excludes urgent or emergency care, and does not generate tests or management.

exact_sections.heading: Appendix 1 - Preanesthesia history and physical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting pertinent medical records, patient interview, directed physical examination, airway evaluation, and lung and heart examination before elective anesthesia.

exact_sections.heading: Preanesthesia testing and pregnancy testing
exact_sections.locator: PDF pages 6-9, testing discussion and Appendix 1 summary
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected testing based on patient and procedure characteristics and offering pregnancy testing to patients of childbearing potential when the result would alter management.

sources.exact_document_title: Practice Guidelines for Preoperative Fasting and the Use of Pharmacologic Agents to Reduce the Risk of Pulmonary Aspiration
sources.population: Healthy patients of all ages undergoing elective procedures requiring anesthesia or sedation, with stated modifying conditions.
sources.clinical_setting: Preprocedure fasting assessment for elective anesthesia or sedation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for preoperative record review, interview, fasting compliance, and aspiration-risk modifiers. Fasting durations, medicine instructions, and management are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Preoperative assessment of fasting status and compliance
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports medical-record review, patient interview, verification of fasting status, and documentation of compliance.

exact_sections.heading: Population and aspiration-risk modifiers
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports noting pregnancy, obesity, diabetes, reflux, obstruction, emergency, and difficult-airway context as assessment qualifiers only.

sources.issuing_organisation: American Urological Association and Society of Urodynamics, Female Pelvic Medicine & Urogenital Reconstruction
sources.exact_document_title: Microhematuria: AUA/SUFU Guideline (2020, Amended 2025)
sources.population: Adults with microhaematuria, with risk, infection, kidney-disease, anticoagulant, gynaecological, and follow-up qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial evaluation, risk-based urological evaluation, and follow-up of microhaematuria.
sources.applicability_note: Partial for a broad haematuria workflow. Exact for selected microhaematuria definition, gross-haematuria history, urinary symptoms, risk factors, urinalysis, blood pressure, creatinine, and qualified follow-up documentation. It does not support automatic cancer conclusions, imaging, cystoscopy, referral, or UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnosis and definition of microhaematuria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports urinalysis with microscopic confirmation and microhaematuria terminology without assigning cause.

exact_sections.heading: Initial evaluation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports history, physical examination, blood pressure, creatinine, gross-haematuria history, irritative urinary symptoms, smoking and occupational exposure, family history, anticoagulant context, urinary infection context, and flank-pain context.

exact_sections.heading: Follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician shared-decision follow-up and reassessment when gross haematuria or new urinary symptoms occur, without automatic investigation or referral output.

sources.exact_document_title: British Association of Dermatologists living guideline for managing people with alopecia areata 2024
sources.version: British Journal of Dermatology 2025;192(2):190–205; corrected and typeset 2025-01-08
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom / adaptable international specialty guidance
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people with suspected or confirmed alopecia areata.
sources.clinical_setting: Community and hospital dermatology settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for alopecia-areata assessment, differential diagnosis, selected investigations, psychosocial impact, and referral; partial for broad diffuse, androgenetic, hormonal, or scarring hair loss and UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Section 3.0 recommendations R1–R10 — assessment, severity, impact, information, and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports full history including site, type, extent, stability, onset, progression, triggers, quality of life, psychological impact, family autoimmune history, photography, severity assessment, and specialist referral criteria.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 5.0–6.2 — presentation, differential diagnosis, and investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Describes patchy or diffuse hair loss, scalp and body distribution, exclamation-mark hairs, nail involvement, trichoscopy, scarring and scaling differentials, hair-pull context, and case-by-case testing when diagnosis or comorbidity warrants it.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations R46–R49 — psychological distress and safety
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented psychological support, formal intervention or referral, and suicide-risk assessment when indicated.

exact_sections.heading: Section 7.0 — management context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led assessment, shared priorities, severity tracking, expectation discussion, treatment selection, monitoring, and patient information without authorising autonomous treatment output.

sources.exact_document_title: British Association of Dermatologists' guidelines for the management of cutaneous warts 2014
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; specialty guidance requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults and children with non-genital cutaneous warts, including plantar lesions and persistent or complicated disease.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care and dermatology assessment and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for wart identity, site, duration, persistence, symptoms, functional or cosmetic impact, immune context, examination, and diagnostic uncertainty. Treatment recommendations and procedure parameters are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Terminology, natural history, site, and persistence
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting wart identity, age, lesion site, duration, persistence, multiplicity, immune context, pain, function, and cosmetic impact.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnosis and complications
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-observed morphology and distribution, diagnostic uncertainty, pain, bleeding, secondary infection, and immunosuppression context without inferring diagnosis or treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: Fungal nail infections
sources.version: BAD patient information leaflet; updated April 2026; review April 2029
sources.population: People with suspected or established fungal infection of fingernails or toenails.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and dermatology assessment and diagnostic-sample documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for fungal nail identity, risk context, colour and thickness change, brittle or crumbly nail, subungual debris, nail lifting or pain, spread, and clinician-obtained nail samples. Medication and liver-test advice are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: What are fungal nail infections and who is affected?
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports onychomycosis and fungal-nail terminology plus affected-nail, spread, trauma, and host-risk context.

exact_sections.heading: What are the symptoms and how is diagnosis made?
exact_sections.locator: official page from line 263 through diagnostic testing
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports yellow, white, or brown discoloration, thickening, brittleness, splitting, debris, roughness, pain, and clinician-obtained clipping or sample for microscopy or culture.

sources.exact_document_title: Plantar warts
sources.version: BAD patient information leaflet; produced June 2022; planned review June 2025
sources.population: People with plantar warts or verrucae; broader non-plantar wart applicability is limited.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and dermatology history and lesion-examination documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for plantar-wart identity, rough or verrucous surface, thrombosed capillaries, lesion number, spread, pain, bleeding, and immunocompromise context. The general warts workflow is broader, the review date has passed, and treatment or procedure passages are not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: What are plantar warts and what do they look like?
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports verruca or wart identity, rough surface, black dots or thrombosed capillaries, location, count, spread, and pain.

exact_sections.heading: Persistence, complications, and when assessment matters
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports persistence, bleeding, rapid change, pain, infection concern, and immunocompromise as documentation prompts without an automatic management conclusion.

sources.exact_document_title: British Association of Dermatologists guidelines for the management of people with rosacea 2021
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; specialty guidance requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults with clinician-assessed rosacea and people with perioral or periocular dermatitis described in the differential-diagnosis section; pediatric evidence is explicitly limited.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care, dermatology, and ophthalmology-linked assessment where ocular symptoms are present.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected perioral and periocular morphology, distribution, symptom, exposure, and differential-documentation fields. The guideline does not cover diagnosis and is not used to generate medicines, treatment, referral, or urgency.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations R3–R4 and background — clinical signs, symptoms, distribution, and ocular context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician documentation of facial distribution, erythema, papules or pustules, flushing, burning or stinging, dryness, edema, and ocular symptoms without inferring diagnosis.

exact_sections.heading: Section 5.9 — perioral and periocular dermatitis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting abrupt or persistent perioral or periocular papules and pustules, redness, scale, lip-margin sparing, itch, burning, soreness, eyelid involvement, and topical, inhaled, or ophthalmic corticosteroid exposure without inferring cause.

sources.exact_document_title: Rosacea
sources.version: BAD patient information leaflet; produced May 2022; planned review May 2025
sources.population: People with rosacea, including cutaneous and ocular features.
sources.clinical_setting: Patient information supporting primary and dermatology history and examination documentation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for rosacea identity, facial distribution, flushing, erythema, papules or pustules, telangiectasia, burning or sensitivity, triggers, and ocular symptoms. The planned review date has passed; treatment passages are not mapped and UAE review remains required.

exact_sections.heading: What is rosacea and what causes it?
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports rosacea identity, central facial distribution, flushing, persistent redness, visible vessels, papules or pustules, and trigger context.

exact_sections.heading: What are the symptoms of rosacea?
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports burning or stinging, skin sensitivity, swelling, ocular irritation, eye symptoms, and vision concerns as clinician-assessed documentation fields.

sources.exact_document_title: British Association of Dermatologists guidelines for the management of people with vitiligo 2021
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; specialty guidance requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults and children with clinician-established or suspected vitiligo; the guideline explicitly does not cover diagnosis.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care and specialist dermatology assessment and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting site, type, extent, stability, speed, triggers, quality-of-life and psychosocial impact, autoimmune history, and medical photography. It is not used to diagnose vitiligo or generate tests, treatment, medicine, referral, or follow-up intervals.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations R1, R3, and R5 — history, extent, stability, impact, and associated disease
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting lesion site and distribution, clinician-established type, extent, stability, speed, triggers, quality-of-life and psychosocial impact, and personal or family autoimmune and thyroid context.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation R8 — photography and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording medical photography and documented affected areas for clinician-led monitoring without creating a response conclusion or fixed interval.

sources.exact_document_title: Shoulder Pain: Diagnosis, Treatment and Referral Guidelines for Primary, Community and Intermediate Care
sources.version: BESS Patient Care Pathways summary; Shoulder & Elbow 13(1):5–11; DOI 10.1177/1758573220984471
sources.population: Adults with common shoulder conditions in primary, community, or intermediate care.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and intermediate musculoskeletal care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for shoulder red flags, broad assessment, subacromial pain, frozen shoulder, glenohumeral arthritis, and instability; international and partial for a generic UAE shoulder-pain workflow.

exact_sections.heading: Shared Decision Making and Urgent Red Flag Referrals for the Shoulder
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires direct clinician-patient decisions and lists infection, unreduced dislocation, tumour, traumatic acute cuff tear, and inflammatory disease as urgent concern contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Subacromial Shoulder Pain — definition and diagnosis
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports rotator-cuff or impingement terminology and history covering hand dominance, occupation, activity, pain location, radiation, onset, duration, aggravating factors, trauma, comorbidities, red flags, and passive external rotation.

exact_sections.heading: Subacromial Shoulder Pain — imaging and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports selected two-view radiography, states ultrasound or MRI is rarely indicated without an agreed specialist pathway, and links persistent symptoms to clinician-arranged referral.

exact_sections.heading: Frozen shoulder, glenohumeral arthritis, and instability summaries
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports range-of-motion, stiffness, function, instability, trauma, recurrent dislocation, and clinician-selected imaging or referral documentation across common shoulder presentations.

sources.exact_document_title: Guidelines on the management of abnormal liver blood tests
sources.population: Children and adults with abnormal liver blood tests, with recommendation-specific adult and paediatric differences.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and secondary care assessment of abnormal liver blood tests.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected result-context, history, adult liver-aetiology-screen, and clinician-referral documentation. The BSG resource page was last reviewed in 2021 with review due in 2025; UAE pathways and patient-specific interpretation require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1 — standard initial liver blood-test panel
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician review of bilirubin and standard liver blood-test components; it does not provide patient-specific interpretation or diagnosis.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 2–3 — prior results and clinical context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Requires review of previous results, past medical history, current condition, the specific abnormal analyte, and clinical context rather than inferring significance from magnitude alone.

exact_sections.heading: Response to abnormal liver blood tests — clinical history
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports age, hepatitis-risk context, jaundice, abdominal pain, weight loss, pruritus, comorbidity, prescribed or non-prescribed drug history, travel or exposure context, and alcohol history.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 4–6 — liver aetiology screen
exact_sections.locator: PDF pages 1 and 10; lines 68–86 and 899 onward
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-reviewed abdominal ultrasound and hepatitis B or C testing in the recommendation-qualified adult liver-aetiology-screen context; investigation choice remains clinician-led.

exact_sections.heading: Response pathway and Recommendation 10
exact_sections.locator: PDF pages 2, 9, and 12; figure 1 and Recommendation 10
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led further investigation or referral based on synthetic failure, suspicious symptoms or signs, analyte pattern, persistence, risk factors, and clinical context without automatically assigning urgency.

sources.exact_document_title: Diagnosis and management of acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding: guidelines from the British Society of Gastroenterology
sources.population: Adults aged 16 years and over presenting acutely to hospital with lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
sources.clinical_setting: Acute hospital assessment and management of lower gastrointestinal bleeding.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for selected acute adult hospital lower-GI-bleeding presentation, initial-assessment, antithrombotic-context, stability, and qualified follow-up fields. Uncomplicated primary-care bleeding, chronic bleeding, diagnosis, treatment, and UAE pathways remain outside scope or require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Scope of the guideline and background presentation description
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines acute adult hospital lower gastrointestinal bleeding as bright or dark red blood per rectum, clots per rectum, or blood mixed with stool, while warning that brisk bleeding should not automatically be ascribed to haemorrhoids.

exact_sections.heading: Management algorithm — initial assessment and Oakland variables
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports routine observations, full history and examination including digital rectal examination, appropriate blood tests, heart rate, systolic blood pressure, haemoglobin, and previous lower-GI-bleeding admission context.

exact_sections.heading: Abstract and recommendations — anticoagulant and antiplatelet context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting whether anticoagulant or antiplatelet treatment is relevant to acute lower gastrointestinal bleeding; medicine interruption, reversal, and restart instructions are deliberately not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1–4 and risk assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of haemodynamic stability, major versus minor bleeding context, and qualified clinician-decided hospital or outpatient follow-up documentation without calculating a score or inserting disposition advice.

sources.exact_document_title: British Society of Gastroenterology guidelines on sedation in gastrointestinal endoscopy
sources.population: People undergoing gastrointestinal endoscopy with recommendation-specific sedation, procedure, age, and comorbidity contexts.
sources.clinical_setting: Endoscopy pre-assessment, procedure, recovery, and discharge.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for selected procedure-context, monitored-recovery, discharge-assessment, follow-up-planning, and safety-instruction documentation. It does not support retrospective diagnosis, pathology interpretation, treatment, or universal post-endoscopy follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: Pre-assessment — indication, intended aim, and procedure context
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the endoscopic procedure context and indication or intended aim as known clinical-record information.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 16–17 — monitoring and complete recovery
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting recovery progress, baseline consciousness or function, vital monitoring, complications during recovery, information exchange, follow-up planning, and discharge advice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 18–19 — suitability for discharge and instructions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports formal documented discharge assessment, recovery from sedation, absence of complications, vital and respiratory status, pain or discomfort review, procedure-appropriate instructions, contact information, and activity restrictions after sedation.

sources.exact_document_title: British Thoracic Society Clinical Statement on chronic cough in adults
sources.population: Adults with chronic cough lasting more than 8 weeks.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and specialist assessment of chronic cough.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult chronic cough assessment and referral; not applicable to children and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Scope and definition of chronic cough
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines chronic cough in adults as cough lasting more than 8 weeks and sets the statement scope.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical history and examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports cough duration and character, sputum, smoking, medicines, occupation and environment, wheeze, rhinitis, heartburn, functional and mood impact, and face-to-face upper-airway, ear and chest examination.

exact_sections.heading: Basic investigations and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports chest radiography, spirometry with reversibility, sputum testing where infection is suspected, selected inflammatory markers, specialist referral for persistent or unclear cough, and upper-airway specialist assessment when indicated.

sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Management Guideline: Keratitis, contact-lens-associated infiltrative
sources.population: Contact-lens wearers with suspected contact-lens-associated infiltrative keratitis.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and first-contact eye care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for contact-lens-associated infiltrative keratitis features and safe lens-care documentation; UAE escalation pathways require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical summary — presentation and differential safety
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports contact-lens use, usually unilateral redness, watering, foreign-body sensation, discomfort, photophobia, peripheral corneal opacity, and the need to distinguish sight-threatening microbial keratitis.

exact_sections.heading: Patient summary — lens cessation, hygiene, and wearing schedules
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented temporary lens cessation, hand and lens-case hygiene, avoidance of overnight wear, wearing-schedule review, and reassessment context.

sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Management Guideline: Corneal abrasion
sources.population: People with suspected corneal abrasion, including trauma- and contact-lens-associated presentations.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and first-contact eye care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for corneal-abrasion history, symptoms, fluorescein assessment, and referral context; UAE pathways require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical information and patient summary — causes and symptoms
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports minor-injury, foreign-body and contact-lens context plus pain, blepharospasm, blurred vision, photophobia, watering, and redness.

exact_sections.heading: Patient summary — assessment and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of the involved area, fluorescein documentation, and referral when injury is deeper, contaminated, or possibly infected; medicine details are not mapped.

sources.exact_document_title: Guidance for Professional Practice Annex 4: Urgency of referrals table
sources.population: People with eye presentations requiring emergency, urgent, or priority referral triage.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and first-contact optometric care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for listed eye red flags and urgency categories; local UAE referral pathways and timing require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Annex 4 — anterior eye and traumatic red-eye referrals
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists severe chemical burns, penetrating injuries, hyphema, embedded corneal foreign body, corneal or lid laceration, blunt trauma, severe abrasion, infective keratitis, and other urgent anterior-eye presentations.

exact_sections.heading: Annex 4 — visual loss and other urgent presentations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists sudden visual loss, sudden vision change, flashes or floaters with retinal signs, retinal detachment, acute diplopia, severe eye pain with nausea or vomiting, pain on eye movement, and new pupil defects.

sources.exact_document_title: Guidance for Professional Practice: Examining patients who present with flashes and floaters
sources.population: People presenting with flashes and/or floaters.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary and first-contact optometric care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for flashes-and-floaters history, examination, referral, and safety-net documentation; UAE pathways require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Principles A286–A291 — history, vitreous and fundal examination, records
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports detailed symptom and risk-factor history, anterior-vitreous review, dilated fundal examination, written information, local-protocol referral, and complete records.

exact_sections.heading: Referral and management A292–A294
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports referral for retinal detachment, symptomatic retinal breaks or tears, pigment or haemorrhage, and safety-net information about new retinal-break or detachment symptoms.

sources.exact_document_title: Clinical Management Guideline: Microbial keratitis (bacterial, fungal)
sources.population: People with suspected bacterial or fungal microbial keratitis.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, first-contact, and specialist eye care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for sight-threatening keratitis features and emergency referral context; UAE pathways and treatment require clinician review.

exact_sections.heading: Clinical and patient summaries — presentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports usually unilateral acute redness, moderate or severe pain, photophobia, watering, discharge, blurred vision, corneal opacity or ulcer, and contact-lens, injury, surgery, exposure, dry-eye, or immune-risk context.

exact_sections.heading: Patient summary — sight-threatening emergency and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Identifies microbial keratitis as sight-threatening and supports urgent ophthalmology documentation; treatment specifics are not mapped.

sources.issuing_organisation: Centre for Perioperative Care and British Geriatrics Society
sources.exact_document_title: Guideline for Perioperative Care for People Living with Frailty Undergoing Elective and Emergency Surgery
sources.population: Adults living with or at risk of frailty who are undergoing elective or emergency surgery.
sources.clinical_setting: Referral, surgical outpatient, preoperative assessment, ward, theatre, recovery, discharge, and community transfer.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for frailty-status documentation, function, cognition, multimorbidity, medicines, nutrition, shared decisions, and review. It is partial for geriatric patients without established frailty; the scheduled review is overdue and UAE pathways remain local.

exact_sections.heading: Standards 3-5 - frailty, comprehensive geriatric assessment, and cognition
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting frailty status for older or at-risk surgical patients, comprehensive geriatric assessment when performed, and cognition assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations for primary care, surgical outpatients, and preoperative assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting frailty score, nutrition and BMI, comorbidities, cognition, medicines, functional status, reassessment, and individualized shared decisions.

sources.exact_document_title: Guideline for Perioperative Care for People with Diabetes Mellitus Undergoing Elective and Emergency Surgery
sources.population: People with diabetes mellitus undergoing elective or emergency surgery, with adult-focused pathways and separate paediatric requirements.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary-care referral, surgical consultation, preoperative assessment, hospital admission, perioperative care, discharge, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for clinician-documented diabetes type, recent review, HbA1c, medicines and devices, BMI, blood pressure, renal function, comorbidities, complications, and an individualized shared perioperative plan. Medicine changes and doses are not mapped; UAE pathways remain local.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations for primary care teams
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting diabetes type, care provider, last review, HbA1c, current medicines and devices, BMI, blood pressure, kidney function, comorbidities, and diabetes complications at referral.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations for surgical outpatients and preoperative assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician review of diabetes, comorbidities, relevant results, medication reconciliation, and a patient-involved individualized perioperative plan without generating medicine changes or doses.

sources.exact_document_title: Surgery and Opioids: Best Practice Guidelines 2021
sources.version: Surgery and Opioids: Best Practice Guidelines 2021
sources.population: People undergoing surgery, including people with chronic pain or preoperative opioid use.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative assessment, perioperative planning, PACU, discharge, pain service, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting chronic pain, current opioid use, medicine and psychosocial or substance history, functional pain assessment, clinician-entered perioperative plan, complex pain review, discharge communication, and pain-service follow-up. Medication changes, doses, and tapering are not mapped; scheduled review is overdue.

exact_sections.heading: Preoperative assessment of pain and opioid use
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting chronic pain, opioid use, medicine history, psychosocial context, alcohol or illicit substance context, function, and specialist involvement.

exact_sections.heading: Perioperative plan and communication
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports a clinician-entered, patient-discussed perioperative pain plan and communication without medicine names, dose, or taper instructions.

exact_sections.heading: Recovery, discharge, pain service, and follow-up
exact_sections.locator: PDF PACU, discharge, and follow-up recommendations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports functional pain assessment, complex pain or pain-service review, discharge communication, and clinician-arranged follow-up without autonomous treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: The international EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI guideline for the definition, classification, diagnosis, and management of urticaria
sources.population: People with acute, chronic spontaneous, or inducible urticaria.
sources.clinical_setting: Allergy, dermatology, and general clinical assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for urticaria definition and classification; detailed management text was not accessible and is not mapped.

exact_sections.heading: Official abstract: definition and presentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines urticaria as a mast-cell-driven disease presenting with wheals, angioedema, or both and distinguishes acute and chronic forms.

exact_sections.heading: Official abstract: impact and scope
exact_sections.evidence_summary: States that chronic spontaneous or inducible urticaria can impair quality of life and work or school performance and that the guideline covers classification, diagnosis, and management.

sources.issuing_organisation: European Dermatology Forum in cooperation with European dermatology and pruritus experts
sources.exact_document_title: European Guideline on Chronic Pruritus: In cooperation with the European Dermatology Forum (EDF)
sources.jurisdiction: European international specialty guidance requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: Adults with chronic pruritus, including dermatologic, systemic, neurologic, psychiatric, drug-related, pregnancy, and older-person contexts.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care, dermatology, and multidisciplinary assessment of chronic pruritus.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for a structured itch history, whole-skin examination, primary versus secondary lesion documentation, contextual review, and clinician-selected investigation documentation. It does not diagnose a cause or generate tests or treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Section 5.1 — patient history and clinical examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting onset, duration, localisation, generalisation, time course, intensity, triggers, associated symptoms, medicines, exposures, personal and family history, whole skin, scalp, nails, oral and anogenital examination, and primary versus secondary lesions.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 5.2–5.4 — diagnostic work-up and differential framework
exact_sections.locator: primary full text diagnostic-work-up sections and tables
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording laboratory or other results already available and clinician-selected investigation context according to history and examination; it does not generate a test panel.

sources.issuing_organisation: European Society of Endocrinology in collaboration with the European Network for the Study of Adrenal Tumors
sources.exact_document_title: European Society of Endocrinology clinical practice guidelines on the management of adrenal incidentalomas
sources.population: People with an incidentally identified adrenal mass, with imaging, hormonal, malignancy-risk, age, pregnancy, frailty, and comorbidity qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Initial adrenal incidentaloma assessment, multidisciplinary review, and qualified follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected imaging, clinical hormone-excess assessment, blood-pressure, hormonal-workup, electrolyte, referral, and follow-up documentation. Surgery, diagnosis, fixed intervals, treatment, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Assessment of malignancy risk by imaging
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports imaging-report review, CT or MRI context, lesion characteristics, qualified follow-up imaging, and multidisciplinary discussion.

exact_sections.heading: Assessment for hormone excess and comorbidity
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinical signs of hormone excess, blood pressure and hypertension, diabetes context, recommendation-qualified hormonal workup, and electrolytes or hypokalaemia context.

exact_sections.heading: Multidisciplinary discussion and follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports qualified clinician-arranged multidisciplinary review, referral, hormonal reassessment, and imaging follow-up without prescribing timing universally.

sources.exact_document_title: Decision making and consent
sources.version: Professional standards guidance, effective 9 November 2020 and updated 13 December 2024
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom professional standards; UAE consent and capacity law governs local use
sources.population: Patients participating in decisions about their care, including decision-specific capacity concerns.
sources.clinical_setting: All clinical settings, including urgent and emergency care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for recording the information exchange, decision, agreed action, decision-maker, no-action decision and a decision-specific capacity assessment. It is not authority for a UAE legal capacity conclusion or involuntary treatment.

exact_sections.heading: Paragraphs 50–55 — Recording decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording the exchange of information leading to a decision, the decision and action agreed, who made or agreed it, and a decision to take no action.

exact_sections.heading: Paragraphs 76–86 — If a patient may lack capacity
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports a time- and decision-specific capacity assessment, presumption of capacity, practical support, and recording the assessment; UK legal rules require UAE adaptation.

sources.issuing_organisation: International Society for Holter and Noninvasive Electrocardiology and Heart Rhythm Society
sources.exact_document_title: 2017 ISHNE-HRS expert consensus statement on ambulatory ECG and external cardiac monitoring/telemetry
sources.population: Adults and selected paediatric patients requiring ambulatory ECG or external cardiac monitoring for symptom-rhythm correlation or defined clinical indications.
sources.clinical_setting: Outpatient and ambulatory rhythm evaluation, monitor selection, recording, analysis, and clinician result review.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting symptoms, event frequency, symptom-rhythm correlation, monitor type and duration context, recording quality, and clinician review of Holter or ambulatory ECG data. It is old and does not generate a rhythm diagnosis, monitor order, treatment, or referral.

exact_sections.heading: Section 3.1.6 - selection of appropriate ambulatory ECG technology
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician selection and documentation of monitoring technology based on symptom frequency, diagnostic power, recording duration, patient acceptance, and recording quality; it does not order a device automatically.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 4.1-4.2 - syncope and palpitations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports symptom history, event frequency, symptom-rhythm correlation, clinical context, and ambulatory ECG review for syncope or palpitations without concluding causation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations table - selection of ambulatory ECG
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting monitor type and duration in relation to symptom frequency and recording window; treatment and diagnosis are outside scope.

sources.issuing_organisation: Heart Rhythm Society, European Heart Rhythm Association, Asia Pacific Heart Rhythm Society, and Latin American Heart Rhythm Society
sources.exact_document_title: 2023 HRS/EHRA/APHRS/LAHRS expert consensus statement on practical management of the remote device clinic
sources.population: People with cardiovascular implantable electronic devices enrolled in in-clinic or remote device follow-up.
sources.clinical_setting: Cardiac device clinics, remote monitoring programs, data triage, alert review, documentation, communication, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting CIED enrollment and connectivity context, scheduled and unscheduled transmissions, data review, alerts, qualified sign-off, communication, and follow-up. It does not interpret device data, program alerts, diagnose an event, or generate a treatment or emergency plan.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 2-3 - remote monitoring considerations, enrollment, connectivity, and follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting device and monitoring enrollment context, connectivity, scheduled follow-up, patient education, and communication responsibilities without asserting device status.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 4-5 - staffing, data triage, review, final sign-off, documentation, and communication
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting that a transmission was triaged and reviewed by qualified staff, signed off, communicated, and linked to an already decided follow-up action.

exact_sections.heading: Section 8 - alert definition, review, and communication
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording received alerts, priority assigned by a qualified clinic, review, communication, and emergency-system limitations without generating alert programming, urgency, diagnosis, or treatment.

sources.exact_document_title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Keloids and Hypertrophic Scars—Japan Scar Workshop Consensus Document 2018
sources.version: Japan Scar Workshop Consensus Document 2018; Burns & Trauma volume 7, article 39 (2019); DOI 10.1186/s41038-019-0175-y
sources.jurisdiction: Japan; international scar-consensus evidence requiring UAE adaptation
sources.population: People with a possible or established keloid or hypertrophic scar; the document highlights greater susceptibility in Asian and African populations.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary care, dermatology, plastic-surgery, and specialist scar assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting scar origin and course, location, symptoms, clinical appearance, growth relative to the original wound, and clinician-assessed scar severity. It does not authorise automatic diagnosis, scoring, biopsy, treatment, procedure, referral, or follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnostic algorithm for pathological scars and clinical diagnosis of keloids and hypertrophic scars
exact_sections.locator: publisher full text, Main context, lines 132–186
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports distinguishing mature scars, hypertrophic scars, keloids, and mimics; documenting scar growth within or beyond the original wound, shape, elevation, redness, hardness, progression, risk context, visual inspection, palpation, and diagnostic uncertainty.

exact_sections.heading: Treatment algorithms at different medical facilities — assessment before treatment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording a clinician-established scar type, subjective symptoms, lesion location, severity context, prior response, movement or tension context, and completed specialist assessment without mapping any treatment instruction.

sources.exact_document_title: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease
sources.population: Adults and children at risk of or living with CKD, with age, cause, GFR, albuminuria, progression, treatment, and referral qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: CKD detection, evaluation, monitoring, medication stewardship, referral, and longitudinal kidney care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for selected CKD, GFR, albuminuria, medication-review, and referral documentation. Diagnosis, treatment, medicine changes or doses, fixed monitoring intervals beyond stated qualifiers, and UAE service pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Detection of CKD and evaluation of chronicity
exact_sections.locator: Practice Points 1.1.1.1–1.1.1.2 and 1.1.3.1–1.1.3.2; summary PDF pages 34 and 54–57
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports GFR and urine-albumin testing, repeat confirmation, prior GFR or albuminuria trends, imaging, history, and the minimum chronicity context without diagnosing CKD from one result.

exact_sections.heading: Evaluation of cause and kidney-function context
exact_sections.locator: Practice Point 1.1.4.1, Figure 8, Table 6, and Practice Point 1.2.1.1; summary PDF pages 34–35 and 61
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports careful medical, family, medicine, urinary, systemic, examination, urinalysis, ACR, electrolyte, and imaging context in clinician-led CKD evaluation.

exact_sections.heading: Evaluation of albuminuria
exact_sections.locator: Practice Points 1.3.1.1–1.3.1.3 and Table 16; summary PDF pages 39 and 75–76
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports ACR, PCR, urinalysis, quantitative confirmation, and interpretation factors including haematuria, menstruation, exercise, and infection.

exact_sections.heading: Monitoring GFR and albuminuria in CKD
exact_sections.locator: Practice Points 2.1.1–2.1.5; summary PDF pages 40 and 81–82
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-arranged GFR and albuminuria monitoring and evaluation of a greater-than-20-percent eGFR change or ACR doubling without mapping fixed legacy intervals.

exact_sections.heading: Polypharmacy and drug stewardship
exact_sections.locator: Practice Point 4.3.1 and Figure 46; summary PDF pages 50 and 135–137
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports periodic medication review, accurate medication lists, adherence, continued indication, interaction, monitoring, reconciliation, and nephrotoxic-medicine context without treatment changes or doses.

exact_sections.heading: Referral to specialist kidney care services
exact_sections.locator: Practice Point 5.1.1 and Figure 48; summary PDF pages 51 and 140–141
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-led referral documentation for qualified sustained GFR decline, significant albuminuria with haematuria, refractory hypertension, persistent potassium abnormalities, and other listed circumstances.

sources.issuing_organisation: Malignant Hyperthermia Association of the United States
sources.exact_document_title: Can Patients with a Suspected Personal or Family History of MH be Safely Anesthetized Prior to Diagnostic Testing for MH Susceptibility?
sources.population: Patients with a suspected personal or family history of malignant hyperthermia susceptibility.
sources.clinical_setting: Preanesthesia history and diagnostic-testing context before formal malignant hyperthermia susceptibility confirmation.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting a suspected personal or family history and incomplete records or testing context. It does not support an automatic diagnosis or anesthesia plan.

exact_sections.heading: Topic; Supporting Evidence — Background and Discussion
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting suspected personal or family malignant-hyperthermia history, unclear or unavailable prior episode details, and diagnostic-testing context without confirming susceptibility or generating management.

sources.exact_document_title: Pituitary incidentaloma: a Pituitary Society international consensus guideline statement
sources.population: Adults with pituitary incidentalomas, with symptom, lesion, endocrine, pregnancy, visual, and surgical qualifiers.
sources.clinical_setting: Pituitary incidentaloma clinical evaluation, endocrine testing, imaging, visual assessment, consultation, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Partial for a broad pituitary-symptoms workflow. Exact only for selected headache, visual, endocrine-symptom, hormone-profile, imaging, visual-field, and referral documentation; diagnosis, hormone replacement, treatment, and UAE pathways remain clinician responsibilities.

exact_sections.heading: Diagnosis and clinical evaluation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports headache, visual loss or blurring, diplopia, cranial-neuropathy, fatigue, and altered-mental-status context as clinician-recorded symptoms or prompts.

exact_sections.heading: Consultation recommendations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports endocrinology or neurosurgery consultation documentation in qualified severe-headache, visual-deficit, hormone-symptom, lesion, or pregnancy contexts.

exact_sections.heading: Pituitary hormone evaluation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports pituitary hormone-profile review and relevant reproductive or prolactin symptom context without inferring hormone deficiency or excess.

exact_sections.heading: Imaging and visual assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports pituitary imaging review, visual fields, and visual-acuity documentation in recommendation-qualified contexts.

sources.issuing_organisation: Royal College of Emergency Medicine and Royal College of General Practitioners
sources.exact_document_title: Discharge to General Practice
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency and primary care; requires UAE pathway adaptation
sources.population: Adult and pediatric patients being discharged directly from an emergency department.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency-department discharge communication with patients and primary care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for recording the clinician-confirmed outcome, treatment plan, information communicated, required follow-up, responsible party, discharge destination and safety-net discussion. It does not make the discharge decision or generate advice.

exact_sections.heading: Summary and Patient Communication
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the clinician-confirmed diagnosis or outcome and plan, patient understanding, specifically required follow-up and safety-net communication.

exact_sections.heading: GP Communication and example discharge-letter headings
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording the presenting complaint, investigations or procedures, outcome, follow-up, responsible party, referral and discharge destination in a discharge handover.

sources.exact_document_title: Management of Domestic Abuse
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency medicine; requires UAE legal and safeguarding adaptation
sources.population: Adults presenting to emergency care where domestic abuse is disclosed or clinically suspected; the text focuses primarily on women.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency department recognition, confidential disclosure, injury documentation and local safeguarding support.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for confidential, clinician-led enquiry where suspicion exists, documentation at disclosure, injury documentation and recording child-welfare concerns. It is not used for universal screening or automatic police, shelter or referral action.

exact_sections.heading: Summary and Recognition of Domestic Abuse
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording clinician suspicion, confidential disclosure opportunity and direct enquiry only when clinically indicated; it does not support universal screening.

exact_sections.heading: Disclosure, injury records, child welfare and support
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports recording disclosure, injuries, child-welfare concerns, and offered contact or support only when actually discussed or completed.

sources.exact_document_title: Invasive Procedures in the Emergency Department
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency medicine; UAE consent, capacity and procedural policy govern local use
sources.population: Adults and children undergoing invasive or high-risk procedures in an emergency department.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency-department invasive-procedure consent, site verification, team briefing, checklist, monitoring and audit.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for recording consent or why it was not possible, site verification where applicable, the proposed procedure and approach, team awareness, post-procedure monitoring and checklist use. It does not authorize or select a procedure.

exact_sections.heading: Summary and procedural safety recommendations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documentation of consent, site verification, proposed procedure and approach, team communication, checklist use and monitoring requirements.

exact_sections.heading: Audit standards and rationale documentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting consent or the reason consent was impossible, site verification and sedation-proforma use when applicable.

sources.exact_document_title: Management of Investigation Results in the Emergency Department
sources.version: Best Practice Guideline, reviewed and updated February 2023
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency medicine; requires UAE governance and pathway adaptation
sources.population: Adults and children whose pathology or radiology investigations were requested or reviewed by an emergency department.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency department result review, communication, handover and follow-up governance.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for recording which test was requested, the result or report review, action taken, responsibility, referral or handover and patient communication. It does not interpret a result or generate management.

exact_sections.heading: Summary and pathology/radiology recommendations — traceable review and responsibility
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports a traceable record of review, action, responsibility, referral and handover for pathology and radiology results.

exact_sections.heading: Operational guidance — result review, patient communication, record and follow-up action
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the specific test, reviewed result or report, communication and clinician-determined follow-up action.

sources.exact_document_title: Mental Health in Emergency Departments: A toolkit for improving care
sources.version: Mental Health in EDs Toolkit, last revised April 2023
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency medicine; UAE law and pathways govern local use
sources.population: Adults and children presenting to emergency departments with mental illness, self-harm risk or mental-health crisis.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency mental-health triage, initial assessment, documentation, observation and liaison working.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting clinician-performed mental-health triage, current risk, agitation, capacity to leave, prior psychiatric history, mental-state examination, safeguarding and liaison referral. It does not predict future suicide or authorise detention, restraint or treatment in the UAE.

exact_sections.heading: Mental-health audit standards for the emergency department
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting triage, current self-harm or suicide risk, leaving risk, capacity for a decision to leave, prior psychiatric history, mental-state examination and referral or follow-up when performed.

exact_sections.heading: Mental Health Triage and Initial Assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting current presenting state, agitation, immediate safety, safeguarding, willingness to accept care, observation level and care-plan status; it explicitly warns triage is not a future-risk prediction tool.

sources.exact_document_title: Procedural Sedation in the Emergency Department
sources.jurisdiction: United Kingdom emergency medicine; UAE credentialing, medicine and facility policy govern local use
sources.population: Adults and children, excluding neonates, undergoing procedural sedation in an emergency department.
sources.clinical_setting: Emergency department procedural sedation governance, pre-sedation assessment, monitoring and post-sedation review.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting use of a sedation proforma, clinician assessment of patient and procedure factors, staffing and environment, monitoring, adverse events and clinician post-sedation review. Pharmacological agents and doses were explicitly excluded from mappings.

exact_sections.heading: Summary, governance and auditable sedation record
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports an auditable sedation proforma, competency, staffing, equipment, adverse-event reporting and recording without selecting a sedative or dose.

exact_sections.heading: Patient and procedure factors, monitoring and post-sedation review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting clinician-assessed patient/procedure factors, airway and aspiration risk, planned level, monitoring, adverse events and clinician review after sedation. Medicine and dose tables are excluded.

sources.exact_document_title: Guidelines for the Provision of Anaesthesia Services for Day Surgery 2025
sources.population: Patients considered for or receiving day surgery.
sources.clinical_setting: Day-surgery selection, preoperative preparation, recovery, discharge, and follow-up.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for suitability, referral, preoperative checks, pain scoring, unplanned admission, discharge protocols, written information, contact, escort, and discharge summaries. UAE criteria require adaptation.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.12 and 1.17 — suitability and preoperative day-of-surgery assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented day-surgery suitability, referral, VTE or pregnancy context, and preoperative checks.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.18–1.27 — recovery, discharge, information, and follow-up
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports pain scoring, unplanned admission, discharge protocol, written information, contact arrangements, escort or support, and discharge summary documentation.

sources.exact_document_title: Guidelines for the Provision of Anaesthesia Services: Chapter 2 — Guidelines for the Provision of Anaesthesia Services for the Perioperative Care of Elective and Urgent Care Patients 2025
sources.population: Elective and urgent surgical patients requiring perioperative anesthesia assessment and care.
sources.clinical_setting: Preoperative assessment, perioperative planning, high-risk review, and referral pathways.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for universal preoperative assessment, previous anesthesia records and alerts, risk domains, high-risk documentation, repeat assessment, medicines, lifestyle, pain, function, and local policies. UAE pathways and thresholds require local adaptation.
